sentences.text: I used to get sick every year at a special time in May, with malaria.
I must have got that someplace over there, and it is a very bad sickness.
There was a small hospital there, very small, and I was there for a month each year at the special time.
The attacks used to come every second day.
I used to shake, develop a temperature, and after that, quiet again.
And that would be every second day with the shaking and cold, and no blanket was good enough to cover up, and then temperature, and then quiet again.
And that's the sickness of malaria.
The medication that I used to get was quinine.
Quinine is a terrible medication because it's bitter.
It's so bitter that no matter what you eat you always taste the quinine.
I developed a little pill for myself.
I used to rub the quinine into a little package of cigarette paper, and swallowed the whole thing through there so it shouldn't affect my throat.
And that's how I developed my own invention-- how to take a pill.
In this particular town-- a small thing and I'm going to make it short, that food was not enough.
I had a bed, and my uncle had a bed.
And one day we decided to sell to the Cossack people the mattress, the cover, of one of our beds and the blanket and the sheet-- we had one sheet.
The mattress was just a cover and straw was inside.
OK.
So when we decided to sell one, we had enough food for the stuff for one day for ourselves.
We ate enough that day, but then we had to sleep together in one small bed for a long, long time, just for one day of food.
Well, that's one of those things that we had to go through.
But I was physically strong otherwise, besides the malaria.
We worked there until the war ended.
I didn't know.
The war, I know-- I found out later that the war ended in 1945.
As a matter of fact, my city was liberated by the Russians in 1944, but I didn't know about that.
And they wouldn't let us out of there anyhow, because they needed whatever help they got from us to work.
So at the end, in '46, the Russian government decided to send all the Polish people back to Poland.
I didn't tell the Russian people that I was born in Rowne, because the city of Rowne, still today, remains a Russian city.
And if I would have said that the city of Rowne was where I was born, they would have sent me back to Rowne, and that means I couldn't get out of Russia anyhow.
I wanted to get out.
So I told them that I was born in the city of Warsaw, which I never saw before.
And the documents that I gave them in 1946-- the beginning of 1946 in the city of Aktiubinsk-- they allowed me to travel to Aktiubinsk.
It wasn't easy to go the 200 kilometers traveling.
Of course, it was winter time.
And I showed them a picture that I had with me, was left of a Zionist organization from before the war.
I have a copy of it that I found in the United States right here, which had epaulets.
And the Russians, at the beginning, were removing those epaulets.
Their soldiers didn't have epaulets in the beginning.
Just later on they put them back.
But being that I showed them with epaulets, they thought that I was Polish.
And there was part of a Polish stamp on the corner of the picture, because that picture was from one of my schools that I had on a certificate.
It was written Polska, or Polish on there, so according to the picture, and looking at me, they decided yes, I am from Poland, and we send you back.
This is how I was able to get out of Russia in 1946, and I traveled to Poland.
It wasn't easy, because they didn't give us any transportation.
We had to hook on the road.
Being that I used to work on the railroad, I knew how to maneuver.
One train I traveled to Kiev from Poltava-- from Poltava to Kiev.
We saw a train that was going towards the city of Kiev, and it was coal.
And we-- a bunch of people from this Donskoe there-- we were together-- we climbed up on that-- on the top of the coal.
And you know the train goes, and the dust from the coal blows.
We came out all black.
When we came to Kiev, we were like a chimney, just as black.
My uncle wasn't there.
There was a different story about my uncle.
In Donskoe, in that same-- I have to go back to this place of Donskoe where he decided to volunteer to the Russian Army.
And he knew about his former wife and child, mother-in-law that was killed, because I told him that.
Up to the time that I met him, he didn't know about his wife, his child-- I have a picture of them and his mother-in-law went to say goodbye to him in 1941 on the station.
The station was bombarded.
They were killed right there, the first day, the day that he left for the Russian Army.
Well, he wanted to take revenge, and in Donskoe he volunteered to the Russian Army, and he was sent to fight.
He was fighting with the Russian Army in the Carpathian mountains in Czechoslovakia, I found out.
After the war ended, he was still in the mountains.
He didn't know the war ended, and that's where he is.
But I'm going to tell you how we found-- he found me again after that, because I didn't know where he was anymore.
He left.
When I came to Kiev, we were traveling on a transport with a lot of Polish people towards Poland.
Well, I didn't know which stations they were going to go through, but one morning they stopped.
I walked out of the train, and I looked out, and I saw a church.
The church looked familiar to me, because it was a Polish church-- a kosciol-- and from the station, I looked at it.
I said to my friends, I said, you know, it looks like we are already in Poland-- in the former Poland-- because I can recognize the churches.
This happened to be my own city, Rowne, which I didn't recognize.
The whole city was completely bombarded.
The only thing they left-- was left in the city was the church, the kosciol-- the Polish kosciol.
There was a big Jewish synagogue was left standing because it looked like a church.
There was the [?
Szabo ?] the Russian Orthodox Church, was also left, which is standing even today.
The rest of the city was completely destroyed.
Only a few buildings were left.
We didn't stop, because that same train-- I couldn't get off to see who was there in the city, because I wasn't going to go to the city.
I was going to go to Poland and Rowne was Russia.
So we continued, unfortunately, further and we came into Poland, somewhere not far from Krakow, we crossed the border.
The Polish soldiers greeted us, and the first thing we found out was anti-Semitism.
They spoke in Polish, which I understood very well.
And they used to say in Polish, oh zyd, zyd.
So you-- we understood what it was and didn't care for us, didn't care for us at all--
--that we were coming back to Poland.
Well, they were saying that they weren't happy to see Jews coming from Russia.
Why don't they stay there?
But we came back, actually not to stay in Poland.
We figured we were going to go someplace else.
From that place, they sent us into Poland, into areas where they could put us, and we went to [?
Oberschlasing. ?] It was sort of-- it used to be a German section of Poland.
And I landed in a city called Bytom-- Beuthen in German.
And as we were opening up the cars, there was a group of Zionist people from Israel came to recruit us to an organization-- to a Zionist organization.
And anybody that was young-- I was probably about-- 1946-- I was 23 years old.
And they took us-- they asked if I wanted to join the Zionist organization.
I said, sure, I'm all alone, I have no family.
I say, why not?
They took my suitcase, whatever I had there, took it right away.
And we went into a-- I joined an organization called Dror, a Zionist organization with 90-- we were all 90 people.
We lived in Bytom, and we were waiting for a transport to go to Palestine at the time.
We had some bad experiences down there in Bytom with the Polish government.
They once wanted to attack us-- all the Jews-- the 90 young Jews-- youngsters in this group of the Dror organization.
Polish soldiers came with the guns.
They wanted us out, and it wasn't easy until the Bricha, it was called.
The Israeli group called Bricha, working from Israel-- they were transporting all the Jews.
They wanted to get them into Israel.
So we had to cross borders.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day they decided to take the whole group out and travel towards Vienna.
We went to Katowice-- it was the town of Katowice, which is not far from the Czechoslovakian border.

And from Katowice, they brought not only the young people from that organization but also their parents, their relatives which lived in Bytom on the outskirts, on the out of this organization building.
And all of us were smuggled towards Czechoslovakia in the middle of the night, which was another experience.
There was a smuggler hired-- a Czechoslovakian smuggler-- knew the road, through the woods, through the border from Poland to Czechoslovakia.
And it was raining that night.
Everybody was full of mud.
We had false documents given by the organization, that made special documents that we were not Jews, but we were Greeks.
Everybody had a name-- a Greek name-- on the document, falsified stamps and everything.
We were hiding these documents when we were on the Polish border into special toothpaste tubes.
But in order for it to be dry-- I have to tell you that the documents were pushed into condoms and tied up.
And then the document was pushed into the toothpaste from the back, folded it up.
So in case the Polish government finds us and they arrest us, they wouldn't know that the real-- the other documents that we had to show on the Czechoslovakian side were in the toothpaste.
If you squeeze the toothpaste, only toothpaste would come out, and you wouldn't know there was a document.
This was one of the tricks that the Bricha had.
When we came over the border, we landed in a special house in [INAUDIBLE] in Czechoslovakia.
There was a house already prepared for all of the group.
We got washed.
We came in in the middle of the night, about 200 people crossing the border.
In the morning, the older people and the children were put on trucks, flat beds, covered with canvas over the top.
They were lying on the trucks.
And this way they traveled from [INAUDIBLE] all the way to Bratislava where there was a hotel and specially prepared a place for them to be accepted.
The young people had to go by train.
And being that [INAUDIBLE] was a tiny little town with not too many people on the train, they decided to let us out two-by-two-- a boy and a girl.
You know, young.
We were-- as I say, we were young.
And we slowly walked towards the station, and we knew when there was going to be a train traveling to Bratislava.
We showed up on the train two-by-two.
Nobody saw us on the street, but when we got to the station, all of a sudden there was about 100 people.
The station manager never saw so many people on the station.
He called the police.
We were arrested-- taken to jail.
They wanted to send us back to Poland.
And we claimed that we were Greeks.
We had our documents already taken out and prepared.
We didn't know the Greek language so we spoke Hebrew.
And the police arrested us-- we were in jail.
But we asked to see a representative from the Jewish organization [INAUDIBLE] in Czechoslovakia, and they worked to let us out.
And they told them that we were not staying in Czechoslovakia, that we were going to Bratislava, and we were getting into Austria, from there Germany, and so on.
So finally they let us go, and we managed to come to Bratislava.
From Bratislava, we went to the border of Czechoslovakia and Austria.
We had a group of 200 people.
The documents-- we had the release from the Czechoslovakian government only for 20 people to let out-- to cross the border.
But the Bricha made it out of 20-- they made 200.
They paid off the guards on the Czechoslovakian side with American cigarettes.
But the didn't look too close.
They let 200 through.
But on the other side of the border it was the Russian part of Vienna, and we had to face the Russian soldiers.
It wasn't easy for me to see them again, because I just left Russia.
Here they had the Russian zone of Vienna.
That's where we got in.
And I didn't talk to them.
And they were also shown the 200, the name-- 200 names, 200 people.
Actually it was 20.
So they gave them vodka, and they let us through into Vienna.
We boarded a streetcar in Vienna, and from there we traveled towards the American zone of Vienna.
And we landed at the Rothschild Hospital from before the war-- Rothschild Szpital in Vienna.
It was-- I was involved as a leader with this organization.
We were going to Israel.
I was going to Israel.
I decided if I have to die, let me die for the cause of Israel, not for the cause of Germany, Poland, or Russia.
So it was easy for me to go.
I was alone.
I lost my family.
I had nobody to go to.
I figured Israel is the best place.
My uncle survived the war and he was in Linz and Bindermichl, in a camp Bindermichl.
He found out, because there was a conference from all the Zionist organizations in that town-- in the camp of Bindermichl.
He asked somebody if anybody saw me, and the leader that was at the conference said yes.
[INAUDIBLE], Tennenbojm or Gresores, or whatever he knew, he's in Vienna in the Rothschild Hospital.
He says, how can I bring him here?
He says well, when we go back, we'll let him know that you're looking for him, and you wait for the next transport that comes from Vienna, to Linz passing by towards Italy.
You wait for him.
He was waiting for me at every station.
And when they told me that he was alive and he was waiting for me in Linz, I left the Rothschild Hospital on the next transport.
They allowed me to go.
I passed by Linz.
He was waiting at the station.
I saw him there in a policeman's uniform, because he was a policeman at that Bindermichl camp.
He looked all right.
He took me down that transport, because the transport was going towards Italy.
And I went with him to this camp.
In order for this particular camp, Bindermichl, it's a DP camp.
It was supported by UNRRA, American UNRRA.
And this particular camp was-- Bindermichl was organized right after the war.
The concentration survivors-- concentration camp survivors-- went to that place.
They took over a lot of buildings.
They decided that all the concentration camp people will meet over there.
And that's where you're going to gather, Bindermichl.
And they established that camp.
In order to get into this camp, you had to be either a relative or somebody-- immediate family.
Otherwise you can't sign in, because the camp was full.
When I came by, he was registered in that camp.
In order for me to get in there, I had to say that he is my brother.
And I had to take his name.
And instead of Tennenbojm or Gresores that I had on the road and other names, I became Gerald Grossman as a brother.
And I lived there in this camp, registered in all Austria, in Linz, with a passport, till I left Europe.
But in between, I had some other things that was happening.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was-- we had-- we didn't know that-- we had-- we knew we had relatives in the United States.
My grandmother lived in the United States.
I was named after my grandfather that lived in the United States, came to Europe, and he died in Europe-- appendicitis.
So he never made it back to the United States.
He only came for a visit.
I'm named after him.
My grandmother died in New Jersey over here in 1941.
She lived in New York.
But she's buried in New Jersey.
My mother had two brothers and a sister that lived here, in New York.
But we didn't know where in New York they live.
We didn't remember any addresses.
And we were looking for a Grossman.
We knew it was Grossman.
They were originally Gresores, but when they came over here they couldn't spell it.
So they gave them the name Grossman.
And that's how Grossman came in.
And I decided, in Linz to look for them.
And I went through plenty of problems.
But the only way I found them is when I put our names into the forwards-- there was a Jewish newspaper forwards-- a little small article there that we are looking for brothers and sister.
And through somebody else that read this piece of paper, showed my uncle, because he was a member of the International Ladies' Garment Union.
And somebody told him that we are looking for him.
And that's how he found out that we were in the camp Bindermichl.
He tried to get us out here.
But it wasn't easy.
He found a man that was going as a commission from the United States to bring tailors over here to the United States and Canada.
He found out that this commission is going to be in Germany.
I lived in Austria.
He wrote me a letter to go to Germany and meet this particular person, because he spoke to him over here.
And he said he's going to do something for me to come to the United States.
I had to cross the border between Austria and Germany, not far from Salzburg, on the border of Salzburg.
As I was crossing the border in the water, I fell.
I slipped and I hit my knee in the water.
And water developed on my knee.
And my whole leg stiffened up.
And I was injured.
I had to go to see this particular person in Munich.
And near Munich, there was a camp-- a DP camp-- Feldafing.
And the director was [?
Gourevic. ?] And he knew about me because he wrote to him.
So with my broken leg-- sort of not broken, full of water-- I had to go into a hospital in this particular DP camp.
They couldn't take me into the hospital, because I wasn't registered in Feldafing.
So I had to get another name.
And the name they gave me from the camp was Leon Swerling.
OK.
Now we've finished tape number four?
